elements: three panels representing world coordinate planes: XY plane, YZ plane, XZ plane
X-axis with gradient scale in first panel (XY plane)
Y-axis with gradient scale in second panel (YZ plane)
Z-axis with gradient scale in third panel (XZ plane)
axis labels on each panel: X, Y, Z
coordinate grid lines on each plane
three arrows on each plane illustrating axis directions

interactions.teaches: Click to focus and learn more about the XY plane and how coordinates are represented there.
Click to focus and learn more about the YZ plane and its coordinate representation.
Click to focus and learn more about the XZ plane and coordinate representation within.

demo_steps.title: Understanding the XY Plane
demo_steps.narration: Here's the XY Plane. It focuses on how the world is represented in two dimensions: X and Y. The grid shows you the positive direction and coordinates aligned on this plane.

demo_steps.title: Exploring the YZ Plane
demo_steps.narration: Let's explore the YZ Plane. This direction shows the world in terms of Y and Z coordinates. It demonstrates vertical and depth representation key to 3D space.

demo_steps.title: Examining the XZ Plane
demo_steps.narration: Now, look at the XZ Plane. This view helps to understand the horizontal and depth elements of world coordinates. It aids in comprehending how direction and distance interplay.

plan.camera_suggestion: Position the camera to display all three planes clearly in a front-facing orthographic view, maintaining correct orientation with equal spacing to evenly represent each axis plane.

inline_constraints.default_view: must maintain individual panel representation for each coordinate plane with correct labels and equal spacing
inline_constraints.visible_ui: compact UI setup next to each plane to maintain visibility without obstructing the view
inline_constraints.explainers: add tooltips for hovering over each plane for extended understanding
inline_constraints.framing: keep proportionate whitespace around the panels as seen in the original layout
inline_constraints.camera: ensure that each panel maintains correct camera aspect ratio and spacing to accurately reflect the coordinate planes

plan.notes: Use a three.js setup capable of housing multiple planes, allowing user interaction between different coordinate views without distorting aspect ratios or label placements.

plan.contextChunk:
\mathbf{A} \mathbf{Y}  = \mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}$$ 

where row $i$ of the matrix ${\bf A}$ contains the
constraint coefficients $\mathbf{a}_i$. The system of equations is
overdetermined ($\mathbf{A}$ has more rows than columns). We can use the
pseudoinverse to compute the solution: 

$$\begin{aligned}
\bf Y = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}$$ This problem can be solved efficiently as the matrix
$\mathbf{A}$ is very sparse (most of the elements are zero).

### Results

@fig-worldCoordinates shows the resulting world coordinates $X(x,y)$,
$Y(x,y)$, $Z(x,y)$ for each pixel. The world coordinates are shown here
as images with the gray level coding the value of each coordinate (black
corresponds to the value 0).

![The solution to our vision problem for the input image of
@fig-edgeLabeling. World coordinates $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ are shown as
images.](figures/simplesystem/worldCoordinates.png){#fig-worldCoordinates
width="100%"}

There are a few things to reflect on:

-   It works. At least it seems to work pretty well. Knowing how well it
    works will require having some way of evaluating performance. This
    will be important.

-   But it cannot possibly work all the time. We have made lots of
    assumptions that will work only in this simple world. The rest of
    the book will involve upgrading this approach to apply to more
    general input images.

Despite that this approach will not work on general images, many of the
general ideas will carry over to more sophisticated solutions (e.g.,

elements: image plane for X coordinates with gradient from black to white
image plane for Y coordinates with height variations represented in gray
image plane for Z coordinates with depth variations in gray
coordinate annotations for X, Y, Z axes

interactions.teaches: Allows viewing from different angles to appreciate how each coordinate contributes to the 3D interpretation.

interactions.label: Toggle View
interactions.teaches: Switches between the 2D gradient images and a simplified 3D model visualizing the coordinate effect.

demo_steps.title: Initial 2D Gradient View
demo_steps.narration: Here are the 2D grayscale representations of the world coordinates. X varies linearly, Y shows height, and Z indicates depth through varying shades of gray.

demo_steps.title: Switch to 3D Interpretation
demo_steps.narration: Now toggle to a 3D view to see these gradients forming a basic 3D shape. This helps in visualizing how 2D gradients are interpreted as 3D coordinates.

demo_steps.title: Explore Coordinate Contribution
demo_steps.narration: Orbit around the visualization to see how each of the X, Y, and Z gradients affects the 3D representation from different angles.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Initial front-facing view with the ability to orbit around smoothly for better exploration.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the arrangement of the original image trio with similar spacing and scale
inline_constraints.visible_ui: only a single compact toggle for the view mode, placed unobtrusively
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips on gradients and 3D model
inline_constraints.framing: match the original crop and layout closely
inline_constraints.camera: start with a head-on view then allow orbiting

interactionPlan.notes: Ensure the 3D transitions are smooth and clear to make the relationship between 2D gradients and 3D shapes obvious.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The source image has three distinct panels with gradients, but the rendered output shows a continuous angular shape.
Labels 'X', 'Y (height)', and 'Z (depth)' are missing from the generated scene.
Different gradient directions in images; the rendered output has gradients creating shadows and highlights, whereas the source has flat gradient transitions.
The rendered scene does not replicate the boxed shapes seen in the Y and Z panels.
evaluation.notes: Shapes and gradients do not match the original; interactivity is limited with only basic toggle.
action_items: Recreate the scene to have three flat planes similar to the source, maintaining distinct panels with appropriate gradient orientation.
Include labels above each panel to match the source figure's annotations.
Improve the interaction to toggle between views that better emulate the 2D panels.

feedback.rationale: The Wrong-Primitives failure mode and low scores indicate a fundamental issue with the conceptual structure needing a revised decomposition to correctly represent distinct panels and gradient orientations.
action_items: Recreate the scene to have three flat planes similar to the source, maintaining distinct panels with appropriate gradient orientation.
Include labels above each panel to match the source figure's annotations.
Improve the interaction to toggle between views that better emulate the 2D panels.
actionItems: Recreate the scene to have three flat planes similar to the source, maintaining distinct panels with appropriate gradient orientation.
Include labels above each panel to match the source figure's annotations.
Improve the interaction to toggle between views that better emulate the 2D panels.

elements: flat plane for X coordinates with gradient from black to white
flat plane for Y coordinates with gradient pattern representing height
flat plane for Z coordinates with gradient pattern showing depth
coordinate labels above each plane: X, Y, Z

interactions.label: 3D View
interactions.teaches: Switches from the 2D gradient images to a simplified 3D model to understand how the gradients relate to 3D visualization.
Displays information about the relationship between gradient intensity and coordinate value.
Allows manipulation of the angles to view the 3D coordinate transformation from various perspectives.

demo_steps.title: View 2D Grayscale Representation
demo_steps.narration: These are the 2D grayscale images representing the world coordinates as gradients. Notice how each panel is distinct for X, Y, and Z coordinates.

demo_steps.title: Switch to 3D Visualization
demo_steps.narration: Toggle to a 3D view illustrating how these grayscale gradients contribute to a blended 3D shape in a vision problem.

demo_steps.title: 3D Exploration of Gradient Contribution
demo_steps.narration: Use the orbit to explore how each gradient image affects the resulting 3D model. Notice what happens to the 3D shape as you view it from different angles.

plan.camera_suggestion: Start with a head-on view aligning with the original 2D image layout, with smooth transitions available to orbit when in 3D mode.

inline_constraints.default_view: must reproduce the original 2D panel layout and scale accurately
inline_constraints.visible_ui: only a single compact toggle for switching to 3D view, placed unobtrusively
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips provide info on gradients and their 3D mapping
inline_constraints.framing: adhere to the original crop and arrangement strictly
inline_constraints.camera: initial view should replicate the original panel layout, allowing seamless orbiting thereafter

plan.notes: Transitions between 2D and 3D must clearly demonstrate the transformation process. The hover interaction provides clarity on each gradient's significance.

Reevaluate the camera setup to maintain correct aspect ratios and spacing.

discrepancies: The original image has three distinct views for X, Y, Z, while the generated output shows a single 3D view.
The gradient directions are incorrect; the original X and Z gradients are horizontal, while Y gradient is vertical.
There are no labels for X, Y (height), Z (depth) in the generated figure.
The generated figure uses toggle buttons which were not present in the original.
evaluation.notes: Main strengths include basic shapes present; weaknesses include incorrect gradient directions, lack of labels, and wrong representation.
action_items: Correct the gradient directions for the X and Z planes in the generated figure.
Add missing labels for X, Y, Z similar to the source image.
Adjust the view to better match the panel layout of the original source.
